text:
University College Hospital

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery

Clinic Date: 21 September 2025

Dr Helen Ward
Riverside Medical Practice
14 Bridge Street, London SE1 2AA

Dear Dr Helen Ward,

Jonathan Miller, DOB: 22-09-1984, Hospital No: J847205 NHS No. 6042198735
Flat 3, 27 Willow Court, London NW1 8QP

Summary/Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy with associated absence seizures and intermittent complex partial episodes; history of recurrent falls related to drop attacks; multiple recent ED attendances for seizure-related triage assessments; mild cognitive slowing postictally; smoker; no current alcohol misuse.

Medication:
- Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily
- Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily
- Clobazam 10 mg nocte as required for clusters (patient-guided use)
- Vitamin D3 800 IU daily, Omeprazole 20 mg daily, Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly

I reviewed him today following several recent ED triage entries that have been forwarded to our service; nursing documentation has been summarised where relevant. He attended with his partner, who corroborated the history. He has been taking medications reliably, using a weekly blister pack. There have been no reported adverse effects beyond occasional daytime fatigue and word-finding difficulty postictally, both short-lived. Weight is stable, and appetite is unchanged. No new focal neurological symptoms. He remains independently mobile and has implemented falls-prevention measures at home after advice from the community team.

Seizures: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per year, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences complex partial seizures with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported. The pattern recorded in recent ED triage notes is consistent with this history, and there has been no escalation in severity or duration. He and his partner maintain a diary and use a shared digital calendar to record events.

Epilepsy classification: He has generalised epilepsy with daily absence seizures and infrequent generalised tonic–clonic seizures, plus superimposed complex partial episodes reported by the patient and witness. No clear precipitating triggers identified; sleep disruption and intercurrent illness remain suspected contributors.

Examination: Alert and oriented, normal speech and language, no focal deficits. Gait steady without assistance. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. No tongue injuries or shoulder tenderness today. Observed mild postural tremor likely medication related.

Investigations: Recent ED point-of-care glucose normal; ECG sinus rhythm. Last routine bloods (June 2025) showed normal FBC, U&E, LFT; vitamin D marginally low—supplementation ongoing. MRI brain (2023) unchanged from prior, no lesion. Routine EEG (2024) with generalised spike–wave discharges.

Plan:
- Continue current antiseizure regimen without changes.
- Reinforce adherence and ongoing use of seizure diary; provide education on recognising clusters and when to use PRN clobazam.
- Community epilepsy nurse to review safety plan and drop-attack precautions; falls clinic referral remains active.
- Check repeat vitamin D and LFT/U&E via GP in three months or sooner if clinical change.
- DVLA guidance already provided and remains applicable given seizure profile.
- Follow-up in neurology clinic in six months, sooner if seizure pattern changes.

Yours sincerely

He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences complex partial seizures with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis:
The letter explicitly describes multiple seizure types with different frequencies:
- "one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per year" → 1 to 2 per year.
- "He has daily absences" → daily implies 1 or more per day; explicit numeric not given but clearly at least daily.
- "complex partial seizures ... every three to four weeks" → approximately 1 per 3 to 4 weeks (~0.25 to 0.33 per week).
- Mentions drop attacks but no explicit frequency beyond being present.
Per Rule 7, select the highest frequency across types. Daily absences represent the highest frequency. Therefore, normalize to "1 per day" as the minimal explicit daily frequency (since exact number per day is not specified).
Not seizure-free, as daily absences are ongoing and no 6-month seizure-free period is indicated.
reference: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per year, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences complex partial seizures with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported
